question: Does Shannon entropy provide a meaningful measure of governance policy complexity — can we compute the entropy of a YAML policy document (treating structural elements as symbols) and use high entropy as a signal that the policy is overly complex?
hypothesis: Shannon entropy computed over the structural tokens of a governance policy (keys, nesting depth, value types) correlates with human-perceived complexity. Policies with entropy above 1 standard deviation from the department mean are candidates for refactoring.
evidence: Shannon entropy is well-established for measuring information density and unpredictability in symbol sequences — YAML structural tokens qualify as a symbol alphabet
Prior work on cyclomatic complexity in software shows formal metrics can approximate human-perceived complexity, suggesting entropy-based metrics are viable for policy documents
YAML's hierarchical structure (keys, nesting, value types) maps naturally to a token alphabet where entropy captures structural variety
Human perception of complexity is subjective and context-dependent — entropy captures structural complexity but may miss semantic complexity (a deeply nested but regular policy may have low entropy)
Cross-validated with GPT-4o-mini: medium agreement — theoretical support strong, but empirical validation needed to confirm correlation with human perception
